originalTitle: The 20-somethings betting big on tech stocks
summary: The BBC hears from people about the risks and rewards of investing in technology shares.
content:
Michelle Huynh, 26, started investing in 2018

In her teens, Michelle Huynh, the eldest daughter of migrant parents in Australia, made a promise to her family that she would become a millionaire by the time she turned 30.

The 26-year-old describes it as "a somewhat silly promise" inspired by the sacrifices her non-English-speaking parents made to raise the family.

But she is trying to make that dream come true by investing her savings in the stock market.

"Times are so different and investing has become a necessity," says Huynh, who works in sales for a tech firm. "It feels like our purchasing power is shrinking. This is the only way to combat that."

This year, the technology-driven surge in stock markets has edged her closer to that goal.

With more than a third of her investments in tech stocks, by the middle of July that part of her savings had jumped this year by 50% - a rise of A$31,000 (£16,100; $21,666).

But those gains have now eased to about A$22,000 as the sector is going through what she calls a "wild moment".

Huynh says she's prepared for the volatility, viewing those investments as a long-term bet.

The rise in tech stocks, led by firms riding the artificial intelligence (AI) boom, has attracted large numbers of ordinary investors, many of them in their 20s and early 30s, even as some analysts warn that the fervour around AI may be overblown.

Retail investors have been caught up in the excitement, which has been fuelled by social media and marketing efforts to draw non-professionals, says Glenn Tan from advisory firm Providend.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq in the US is up by about 10% this year, while Japan's Nikkei 225 has risen by more than 20%.

But many tech stocks have seen big swings - both up and down.

That volatility is most apparent in South Korea. Seoul's Kospi index, which includes tech heavyweights like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics, has jumped by more than 50% since January.

The rally has attracted an army of retail investors, known locally as "ants", which has helped fuel volatile trading.

"I could maybe count with my hands the number of people who aren't investing today," South Korean investor U Chan Lee says. "Even stay-at-home mothers, like my mum, who has never been interested in the stock market, are now interested."

But the Kospi has seen some dramatic moves. Since hitting a record high of more than 9,000 points in June it has plunged to around 6,500.

Trading on the benchmark index has been halted seven times this year under a mechanism called the circuit breaker, designed to calm panic selling after it fell by 8%.

Those slides have raised concerns over people who have borrowed money to invest in stocks, leading to South Korean authorities taking action to curb the practice.

Lee, 30, sold many of his shares last year when the Kospi surged, concerned the market was becoming "too overheated". Since then, he has shifted to buying stocks when they fall and selling them when they rise a few days later.

Is there an AI stock market bubble, and is it ready to burst?

The FTSE 100 has hit a record high. Is now the time to start investing?

The swings reflect the hazards of investing in tech, some analysts warn.

As governments and companies pour hundreds of billions of dollars into developing AI, sceptics are asking whether the technology will be profitable enough to justify such huge investments.

People often make bets on "optimistic outcomes" or "the most visible winners" that are not based on a business's profitability, analyst Lale Akoner from investment firm eToro says.

Retail investors often view drops in shares as buying opportunities, treating each sell-off as a "test of conviction", she says, but they need to be aware of "how painful valuation resets can be".

Jacqueline Choi, from South Korea, says she missed out on bigger gains by not investing more ahead of the Kospi's rally, and was forced to sell Hyundai Motor and Samsung Electronics shares when she needed money.

"Why didn't I go in and buy those SK Hynix stocks or extra Samsung stocks with all of my savings?" the 28-year-old now wonders.

Earlier this month, computer chipmaker SK Hynix debuted in New York, raising $26.5bn to become the largest ever US listing by a foreign firm.

Some of Choi's friends have used thousands of dollars of their savings to invest in stocks.

"I should really invest more, knowing that investing can earn you so much more than your everyday job," she says.

Jacqueline Choi says she regrets not putting more money into shares

Singaporean business student Shyan Lim is a firm believer in AI-related shares, putting about three-quarters of his savings into tech stocks.

The 24-year-old says there have been plenty of "uneasy" days when his investments plunged by as much as 10%, but he is willing to take his chances.

Overall, his bets have worked so far. In October, he invested 23,000 Singapore dollars (£13,185; $17,845) in chipmakers Intel and Micron. Those shares are now worth about 100,000 Singapore dollars.

"It feels like I'm one step closer to retirement," Lim says. "While I'm still young I think I can take the risk. I probably won't take such positions when I'm older."

With more than half of his investments in tech, George Lee, who recently graduated, says he is well aware of the lack of resources and other disadvantages that ordinary traders can face.

"Investing doesn't just come down to luck, though it does play a part," Lee says.

"As long as the fundamentals of a company don't change, I'm ready for the swings since I have time to let my shares run."

Around three quarters of Shyan Lim's shares are in tech companies

But not everyone is willing to bet it all on tech.

Singaporean student Ayush Deb says the sector accounts for only about a third of his investments. The 23-year-old says even that has been difficult for him, recalling how in June his memory chip-focused investments fell by more than 10% in just one day .

The same day, people on investment
chineseTitle: 20 多岁的年轻人大举押注科技股
chineseSummary: 英国广播公司 (BBC) 向人们讲述了投资科技股的风险和回报。
chineseContent:
Michelle Huynh，26 岁，2018 年开始投资

米歇尔·黄 (Michelle Huynh) 是澳大利亚移民父母的长女，她在十几岁的时候就向家人承诺，自己在 30 岁时将成为百万富翁。

这位 26 岁的女孩形容这是“一个有点愚蠢的承诺”，她的灵感来自于她的非英语父母为养家糊口所做的牺牲。

但她正试图通过将积蓄投资于股市来实现这个梦想。

“时代已经如此不同，投资已成为一种必需，”在一家科技公司从事销售工作的 Huynh 说。 “感觉我们的购买力正在萎缩。这是应对这种情况的唯一方法。”

今年，科技驱动的股市飙升让她更接近这一目标。

她三分之一以上的投资都投资于科技股，到 7 月中旬，她的部分储蓄今年增加了 50%，增加了 31,000 澳元（16,100 英镑；21,666 美元）。

但由于该行业正在经历她所说的“疯狂时刻”，这些涨幅现已回落至约 22,000 澳元。

Huynh 表示，她已做好应对波动的准备，并将这些投资视为长期投资。

在人工智能 (AI) 热潮中，科技股的上涨吸引了大量普通投资者，其中许多人年龄在 20 多岁和 30 岁出头，尽管一些分析师警告称，围绕人工智能的热情可能被夸大了。

咨询公司普罗维登 (Providend) 的格伦·谭 (Glenn Tan) 表示，社交媒体和吸引非专业人士的营销活动加剧了散户投资者的兴奋。

以科技股为主的美国纳斯达克指数今年上涨了约 10%，而日本日经 225 指数则上涨了 20% 以上。

但许多科技股都出现了大幅波动——既有上涨也有下跌。

这种波动在韩国最为明显。首尔的 Kospi 指数（包括 SK 海力士和三星电子等科技巨头）自 1 月份以来已上涨了 50% 以上。

此次涨势吸引了大批散户投资者，在当地被称为“蚂蚁”，这加剧了交易的波动。

韩国投资者 U Chan Lee 表示：“我也许可以用手数一数今天没有投资的人数。” “即使是全职妈妈，比如我妈妈，以前对股市不感兴趣，现在也感兴趣了。”

但综合股价指数却出现了一些戏剧性的走势。自6月份创下9000多点的历史新高以来，已跌至6500点左右。

今年，该基准指数的交易已在名为“熔断机制”的机制下暂停七次，旨在平息该指数下跌 8% 后的恐慌性抛售。

这些下滑引发了人们对借钱投资股票的担忧，导致韩国当局采取行动遏制这种做法。

30 岁的李去年在韩国综合股价指数飙升时出售了许多股票，因为他担心市场变得“过于过热”。从那时起，他开始在股票下跌时买入，并在几天后上涨时卖出。

人工智能股市是否存在泡沫，是否已准备好破裂？

富时 100 指数创下历史新高。现在是开始投资的时候了吗？

一些分析师警告说，这种波动反映了投资科技的风险。

随着各国政府和企业投入数千亿美元开发人工智能，怀疑论者开始质疑这项技术是否能带来足够的利润来证明如此巨额投资的合理性。

投资公司 eToro 的分析师 Lale Akoner 表示，人们经常对“乐观结果”或“最明显的赢家”进行押注，而这些赌注并非基于企业的盈利能力。

她表示，散户投资者通常将股价下跌视为买入机会，将每次抛售视为“信念的考验”，但他们需要意识到“估值重置可能是多么痛苦”。

来自韩国的杰奎琳·崔 (Jacqueline Choi) 表示，由于在韩国综合指数上涨之前没有进行更多投资，她错过了更大的收益，并在需要资金时被迫出售现代汽车和三星电子的股票。

“我为什么不用我所有的积蓄去购买那些 SK 海力士股票或额外的三星股票呢？”这位 28 岁的年轻人现在想知道。

本月早些时候，计算机芯片制造商 SK 海力士在纽约首次亮相，筹集了 265 亿美元资金，成为有史以来最大的外国公司在美国上市的交易。

崔的一些朋友用了数千美元的积蓄来投资股票。

“我真的应该进行更多投资，因为我知道投资可以让你赚到的钱比你的日常工作多得多，”她说。

蔡杰奎琳表示后悔没有投入更多资金入股

新加坡商学院学生 Shyan Lim 是人工智能相关股票的坚定信徒，他将大约四分之三的积蓄投入了科技股。

这位 24 岁的年轻人表示，有很多“不安”的日子，他的投资暴跌了 10%，但他愿意冒险。

总的来说，到目前为止他的赌注是有效的。 10 月份，他向芯片制造商英特尔和美光投资了 23,000 新元（13,185 英镑；17,845 美元）。这些股票现在价值约10万新元。

“感觉我离退休又近了一步，”林说。 “虽然我还年轻，但我认为我可以承担这个风险。当我长大后，我可能不会担任这样的职位。”

最近毕业的乔治·李 (George Lee) 一半以上的投资都投资在科技领域，他表示，他很清楚普通交易者可能面临的资源缺乏和其他劣势。

“投资并不仅仅取决于运气，尽管它确实发挥了一定作用，”李说。

“只要公司的基本面不发生变化，我就准备好应对波动，因为我有时间让我的股票运行。”

Shyan Lim 约四分之三的股份属于科技公司

但并不是每个人都愿意将全部赌注押在科技上。

新加坡学生阿尤什·德布 (Ayush Deb) 表示，该行业仅占他投资的三分之一左右。这位 23 岁的年轻人表示，即使这样对他来说也很困难，他回忆起 6 月份他以存储芯片为重点的投资在短短一天内就下跌了 10% 以上。

当天，投资人
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
